meta.title: Who's to Say Sports | Who's to Say ? Foundation
meta.description: Sports, fitness, fan culture, media, and personal wellness for people who want to participate, connect, and grow through the power of sports.

hero.title: Who's to Say Sports
hero.subtitle: Sports, fitness, fan culture, media, and personal wellness — open to people who want to participate, connect, and grow through the power of sports.
hero.bestFor: People who enjoy sports, fitness, team culture, community events, sports media, or personal wellness goals — including individuals with IDD who benefit from flexible support, planning, and encouragement.

blocks.html: Who's to Say Sports is a community-based program for individuals who love sports in any form — playing, training, cheering, storytelling, volunteering, filming, interviewing, or simply being part of a team environment.

The program is designed for the general public and intentionally inclusive of individuals with intellectual and developmental disabilities. Participants may join as athletes, fans, media creators, team helpers, wellness learners, or community members looking for a positive way to build confidence, routine, communication, health, and belonging.
Who's to Say Sports uses sports and wellness as real-world settings for personal growth.

For some participants, that may mean joining inclusive sports programs, attending games, or helping with team activities. For others, it may mean building a personal workout routine, learning how to talk about sports, recording short commentary clips, practicing interviews, or creating content around a favorite team.
The goal is not only participation. The goal is helping people build confidence, independence, communication, healthy habits, and meaningful community connection.
Participants may explore opportunities such as:

items: Inclusive sports programs, clinics, practices, or recreational activities
Challenger, alumni, adaptive, or community-based sports involvement
Team helper, manager, sideline, spirit, or media-support roles
Group outings to games, fields, gyms, tournaments, or sports-related events
Watch parties, fan nights, sports conversations, and community gatherings

blocks.html: Who's to Say can help participants prepare for these experiences, understand expectations, build confidence, and reflect afterward on what went well and what they want to try next.
Sports Path may also include personal wellness planning for participants who want to build healthier routines. This can include:

items: Creating a simple personal fitness goal
Building a weekly movement or workout plan
Tracking progress in a positive, nonjudgmental way
Practicing gym readiness, stretching, hydration, nutrition awareness, and recovery habits
Learning how exercise supports energy, confidence, focus, and independence
Connecting fitness goals to sports interests, employment goals, or daily living routines

blocks.html: This is not medical treatment, physical therapy, or clinical health care. Participants should follow guidance from their doctor, therapist, family, or approved care team when needed.
The Who's to Say role is to support motivation, planning, routine-building, encouragement, and real-world follow-through.
Participants who enjoy talking about sports, making videos, or creating content can explore sports media skills such as:

items: Recording short game recaps or "fan takes"
Interviewing teammates, coaches, family members, or friends
Filming highlights or behind-the-scenes moments on a phone
Creating simple sports commentary, podcast, or social media-style segments
Reviewing clips and practicing clear communication
Building a personal sports voice, brand, or storytelling style

blocks.html: This gives participants another way to belong in sports culture, even if they do not want to compete on the field.
Sports naturally create opportunities to practice real-world skills. Participants may work on:

items: Preparing for an outing or workout
Speaking on camera or in conversation
Reflecting on goals, choices, and progress

blocks.html: These skills can support broader independence, community inclusion, employment exploration, and personal confidence.
Participants may choose from activities such as:

items: Walking, stretching, strength, or conditioning routines
Watch parties and sports discussion groups

blocks.html: Who's to Say Sports is designed to help participants build:
Who's to Say Sports is open to people who are interested in sports, fitness, wellness, fan culture, or media storytelling.

The program is especially supportive for individuals who may benefit from encouragement, structure, flexible pacing, and inclusive community settings, including young adults and adults with intellectual and developmental disabilities.
Participants do not need to be competitive athletes. They only need an interest in sports, movement, wellness, media, or being part of a positive community.

getStarted.title: Get Started
getStarted.prompt: Interested in sports, fitness, wellness, or sports storytelling? Who's to Say Sports can help you explore what role sports might play in your life — whether you want to move more, join a team, attend events, create media, build confidence, or become more connected to your community.

blocks.html: Who's to Say Sports can be offered through individual sessions, small groups, community outings, media projects, wellness planning, or partnerships with local teams and volunteers.
A participant's experience may include:

items: A personal sports or wellness interest conversation
Preparation before a workout, event, practice, or outing
Participation in a sports, fitness, fan, or media activity
Optional media creation, journaling, or progress tracking
Continued planning for the next step

blocks.html: Support is flexible. One participant may want help staying connected to a team. Another may want to start working out consistently. Another may want to become a sports podcaster, commentator, team photographer, or fan reporter.
Who's to Say Sports can support individualized goals related to community inclusion, communication, health routines, employment exploration, social development, and independence.
When applicable, supports may include planning, reminders, skill-building, reflection, guided participation, and personal development connected to sports, fitness, wellness, or sports media.

Funding, eligibility, and service structure depend on each individual's plan, goals, and approval process. Goods & Services or other support funding should be used only for eligible individualized supports, not for general entertainment, admission fees, unrelated purchases, or supervision that is not part of an approved support plan.
Who's to Say is committed to clear documentation, appropriate boundaries, and programs that serve the broader community while remaining accessible and meaningful for individuals with IDD.
